Author: Smith A
Title: Laparoscopic repair of anorectal malformations in neonates: a systematic review
Abstract Note: This meta-analysis evaluated outcomes of laparoscopic versus open repair. Methods: We searched PubMed and Scopus. Results: 12 RCTs included. Laparoscopic repair showed better continence rates (OR 1.8, 95% CI 1.2–2.7).

Author: Jones B
Title: Posterior sagittal anorectoplasty outcomes: 10-year follow-up
Abstract Note: Long-term follow-up of PSARP in 150 children. Fecal continence achieved in 72%. Urinary complications in 8%.

Author: Smith A
Title: Laparoscopic repair of anorectal malformations in neonates: a systematic review
Abstract Note: Duplicate record — same DOI as ZK001.

Author: Chen C
Title: Animal model study of intestinal microbiome in rats after colorectal surgery
Abstract Note: Rat model experiment. No clinical outcomes reported. Not relevant to human pediatric surgery.

Author: Kumar D
Title: Case report: rare presentation of Hirschsprung disease mimicking ARM
Abstract Note: Single case report. No comparative data. Case series excluded per protocol.

Author: Nguyen E
Title: Transperineal ultrasound for classification of anorectal malformations: prospective cohort
Abstract Note: Prospective cohort of 45 neonates. Transperineal ultrasound showed 94% sensitivity for high vs low ARM classification. Compared with invertogram.

Author: Wilson F
Title: Quality of life after ARM repair: patient-reported outcomes
Abstract Note: Patient-reported QoL study. n=89. PedsQL scores significantly lower in ARM patients vs controls.

Author: Lee G
Title: Colostomy complications in neonates with anorectal malformations: systematic review
Abstract Note: Systematic review of colostomy morbidity. 15 studies included. Prolapse rate 12%, wound infection 8%.

Author: Brown H
Title: Fecal continence after pull-through for Hirschsprung disease
Abstract Note: Hirschsprung disease — not ARM. Different PICO. Excluded.

Author: Patel I
Title: Robot-assisted repair of ARM: preliminary experience
Abstract Note: Feasibility study. n=8. Short follow-up. Minimal data on functional outcomes.
